Mark Scheme — Predicted Paper 1 (Moderate) | 4EC1/01


                                Mark Scheme (Predicted)
                                            MODERATE
                                Pearson Edexcel International GCSE
                                     in Economics (4EC1) Paper 01
                          Paper 1: Microeconomics and Business Economics

General Marking Guidance
• All candidates must receive the same treatment.
• Mark schemes should be applied positively. Candidates must be rewarded for what they have shown they
can do rather than penalised for omissions.
• Examiners should mark according to the mark scheme, not according to their perception of where grade
boundaries may lie.
• All marks on the mark scheme are designed to be awarded. Award full marks if the response matches the
mark scheme; award zero if the response is not worthy of credit.
• Crossed out work should be marked UNLESS the candidate has replaced it with an alternative response.


                                                    Page 1
                                                         Mark Scheme — Predicted Paper 1 (Moderate) | 4EC1/01


Question   Which one of the following values shows price inelastic demand?               Mark
number     Answer
1(a)       AO1 1 mark                                                                         (1)
           The only correct answer is:
           A –0.3
           B is not correct because this is unitary elasticity
           C is not correct because demand is price elastic
           D is not correct because this is perfectly price elastic demand


Question   A country has a minimum wage of $8.50 per hour. If a worker works             Mark
number     40 hours in a week, the lowest weekly wage the worker can earn is
           Answer
1(b)       AO2 1 mark                                                                         (1)
           The only correct answer is:
           C $340.00
           A is not correct because this is only one hour of labour
           B is not correct because this is 20 hours at minimum wage
           D is not correct because this is 80 hours at minimum wage


Question   What is meant by the term free market economy?                                Mark
number     Answer
1(c)       AO1 2 marks                                                                        (2)
           Award up to 2 marks for a correct definition
           An economy in which private individuals/firms (1) make decisions
           about the allocation of resources with no/minimal government
           intervention (1)
           Accept any other appropriate response

number     economy.

           Award 1 mark for a correct example
           • Farmer / Fisherman / Miner / Oil driller / Logger
           Accept any other appropriate response

number     Answer

Question   Calculate, to two decimal places, the price elasticity of demand (PED)      Mark
number     for a good if the price rises by 5% and the quantity demanded falls by
           10%. You are advised to show your working.
           Answer
1(f)       AO2 2 marks                                                                      (2)
           Award 1 mark for showing correct calculation
           –10% ÷ 5% (1)
           Award 1 mark for correct PED
           = –2.00 (1)
           Award 2 marks if PED is correctly calculated as –2 or –2.00, even if no
           calculations are shown
           Do not award marks for the formula alone


Question   Draw the likely effects on the market for coffee if consumer incomes        Mark
number     rise.
           Answer
1(g)                                                                                        (3)


           AO2 3 marks
           Award 1 mark for a rightward shift of the demand curve, labelled (e.g.
           D1)
           Award 1 mark for a higher equilibrium price, labelled (e.g. P1)
           Award 1 mark for a higher equilibrium quantity, labelled (e.g. Q1)


Question   Explain one possible opportunity cost for Ahmed of this decision.           Mark
number     Answer


                                              Page 3
                                                            Mark Scheme — Predicted Paper 1 (Moderate) | 4EC1/01

1(h)           AO2 3 marks                                                                       (3)
               Award 1 mark for identifying a relevant opportunity cost
               Award 1 mark for developing the opportunity cost
               Award 1 mark for the response being in context
               • One opportunity cost is that Ahmed may not be able to buy new
               clothes (1) because he does not have enough money to buy both a
               bicycle and clothes (1), so he has to sacrifice the next best alternative
               when using the money to buy the bicycle (1)
               Accept any other appropriate response


Question       With reference to the data above and your knowledge of economics, analyse two
number         possible factors Maria should consider when deciding to employ a third baker.
               Indicative content
1(i)                                        AO2 (3 marks) / AO3 (3 marks)
                • Factors influencing firm decisions to employ more labour include: demand for the
                  product, the cost of labour (wages), productivity of new workers, availability of
                  skilled workers and the aims of the firm.
                • Maria has seen demand for bread grow over the past year, which suggests a third
                  baker may be needed to meet the additional demand.
                • Employing a third baker would raise Maria's total costs, so she needs to consider
                  whether her revenue from the extra output would be higher than the extra wage
                  cost.
                • If skilled bakers are available locally in Lima, recruitment will be easier and may
                  raise productivity at the bakery.
                • Division of labour may allow her existing two workers to specialise (e.g. one in
                  baking, one in selling) with the new baker, raising efficiency and lowering average
                  cost per loaf.


Level      Mark         Descriptor
           0            No rewardable material.
Level 1    1–2           • Demonstrates basic knowledge and understanding by developing
                           relevant points. Limited application of economic terms, concepts,
                           theories and calculations (AO2).
                         • Information presented will lack selectivity and organisation.
                           Interpretation of economic information will be limited, with a lack of
                           analysis of issues (AO3).

Level 2    3–4           • Demonstrates partial knowledge and understanding by developing
                           relevant points. Partial application of economic terms, concepts, theories
                           and calculations (AO2).
                         • Information presented will demonstrate some selectivity and
                           organisation. Interpretation of economic information will be good, with
                           some analysis of issues (AO3).

Level 3    5–6           • Demonstrates clear knowledge and understanding by developing relevant
                           points. Appropriate application of economic terms, concepts, theories

                  and calculations (AO2).
                • Information presented will demonstrate excellent selectivity and

                  with a thorough analysis of issues (AO3).

Question   Which one of the following diagrams shows excess demand?                      Mark
number     Answer
2(a)       AO2 1 mark                                                                         (1)
           The only correct answer is:
           A
           B is not correct because this shows excess supply
           C is not correct because this shows a shift of supply
           D is not correct because this shows a movement along the demand
           curve


Question   Which one of the following is a feature of perfect competition?               Mark
number     Answer
2(b)       AO1 1 mark                                                                         (1)
           The only correct answer is:
           C There are many small firms
           A is not correct because this is a feature of monopoly
           B is not correct because firms sell homogeneous (identical) products in
           perfect competition
           D is not correct because there are low barriers to entry in perfect
           competition


Question   Calculate the percentage change in quantity demanded for a normal             Mark
number     good if the income elasticity of demand (YED) is 0.8 and income rises
           by 15%.
           Answer
2(c)       AO2 2 marks                                                                        (2)
           Award 1 mark for showing the correct calculation
           15% × 0.8 (1)
           Award 1 mark for correct change in quantity demanded
           = 12% (1)
           Award 2 marks if the change in quantity demanded is correctly
           calculated as 12%, even if no calculations are shown
           Do not award marks for the formula alone


Question   What is meant by the term external costs?                                     Mark
number     Answer
2(d)       AO1 2 marks                                                                        (2)
           Award up to 2 marks for a correct definition
           Costs that fall on third parties (1) as a result of economic activity /
           production or consumption (1)


                                                Page 6
                                                       Mark Scheme — Predicted Paper 1 (Moderate) | 4EC1/01

           Accept any other appropriate response


Question   Describe one feature of monopolistic competition.                           Mark
number     Answer
2(e)       AO1 2 marks                                                                      (2)
           Award 1 mark for reference to the feature and 1 mark for
           development
           Many firms (1) each with a small share of the market (1)
           OR
           Differentiated products (1) so firms can compete on branding/quality
           as well as price (1)
           OR
           Low barriers to entry and exit (1) so new firms can enter if short-run
           profits are made (1)
           Accept any other appropriate response


Question   Explain one reason why Leila may want to increase wages rather than         Mark
number     maximise profits.
           Answer
2(f)       AO2 3 marks                                                                      (3)
           Award 1 mark for identifying a relevant reason
           Award 1 mark for developing the reason
           Award 1 mark for the response being in context
           • Managers may have different objectives to owners (1). Leila may
           wish to keep good workers and reduce staff turnover, so raising wages
           could retain staff (1) even though higher wages reduce the café's
           profits (1)
           • Managers may pursue social responsibility (1) such as paying a fair
           wage, which improves staff motivation (1) even if it lowers the café's
           profits (1)
           Accept any other appropriate response


Question   Assess the effectiveness of government subsidies when encouraging consumption of
number     merit goods such as electric vehicles.
           Indicative content
2(g)                          AO2 (3 marks) / AO3 (3 marks) / AO4 (3 marks)
            • Merit goods are goods that are underconsumed in a free market because they
              generate positive externalities.
            • A subsidy is a grant from the government that reduces the cost of production
              and/or the price paid by consumers.
            • Norway's EV subsidies have lowered the effective price of electric cars, increasing
              demand — 82% of new car sales in 2023 were fully electric, showing the policy
              has changed consumer behaviour.


                                              Page 7
                                                         Mark Scheme — Predicted Paper 1 (Moderate) | 4EC1/01

              • Higher EV use reduces air pollution and CO₂ emissions, correcting under-
                consumption of the positive externality.
              • Therefore, subsidies have been effective in encouraging consumption of this
                merit good.
              • AO4
              • However, subsidies are expensive and create an opportunity cost — money spent
                on EV subsidies could have been used for healthcare or education.
              • Subsidies can be captured by higher-income consumers who would have bought
                an EV anyway, reducing value for money.
              • Effectiveness depends on the size of the subsidy and the availability of
                complementary infrastructure (e.g. charging points).
              • Alternative policies such as regulation, carbon taxes on petrol cars, or information
                campaigns might achieve the same outcome at lower cost.


Level     Mark        Descriptor
          0           No rewardable material.
Level 1   1–3          • Demonstrates basic knowledge and understanding by developing
                         relevant points. Limited application of economic terms, concepts,
                         theories and calculations (AO2).
                       • Information presented will lack selectivity and organisation.
                         Interpretation of economic information will be limited, with a lack of
                         analysis of issues (AO3).
                       • Only offers one viewpoint, meaning there is an unbalanced and
                         incomplete evaluation, showing limited understanding and awareness
                         (AO4).

Level 2   4–6          • Demonstrates partial knowledge and understanding by developing
                         relevant points. Partial application of economic terms, concepts, theories
                         and calculations (AO2).
                       • Information presented will demonstrate some selectivity and
                         organisation. Interpretation of economic information will be good, with
                         some analysis of issues (AO3).
                       • Offers more than one viewpoint but the argument may lack balance,
                         leading to an evaluation that may not demonstrate full understanding
                         and awareness (AO4).

Level 3   7–9          • Demonstrates clear knowledge and understanding by developing relevant
                         points. Appropriate application of economic terms, concepts, theories
                         and calculations (AO2).
                       • Information presented will demonstrate excellent selectivity and
                         organisation. Interpretation of economic information will be excellent,
                         with a thorough analysis of issues (AO3).
                       • Offers more than one viewpoint. The argument is well balanced and
                         coherent, leading to an evaluation that demonstrates full understanding
                         and awareness (AO4).

number     Answer
3(b)       AO1 1 mark                                                                        (1)
           The only correct answer is:
           B Total revenue − total costs
           A is not correct because this does not give profit
           C is not correct because this gives total revenue, not profit
           D is not correct because this does not give profit

Question       Analyse the possible advantages for Café Primo of using specialisation at its coffee
number         shops.
               Indicative content
3(d)                                        AO2 (3 marks) / AO3 (3 marks)
                • Specialisation (division of labour) involves breaking down the production process
                  into smaller tasks, with each worker focused on one task.
                • In a Café Primo shop, baristas make drinks, cashiers take payments, and cleaners
                  maintain hygiene. This allows each worker to become skilled at their specific role.
                • By specialising, productivity rises because workers complete their tasks more
                  quickly, allowing Café Primo to serve more customers per hour.
                • This raises revenue and can lower average costs per drink, supporting the firm's
                  rapid expansion to 40 new branches.
                • Time is saved because staff do not switch between tasks, creating further
                  efficiency gains.

                           • Information presented will demonstrate some selectivity and

Question       Assess whether demand is likely to be more price inelastic for cigarettes than for
number         smartphones.
               Indicative content
3(e)                              AO2 (3 marks) / AO3 (3 marks) / AO4 (3 marks)
                • Price elasticity of demand (PED) measures the responsiveness of demand to a
                  change in price.
                • Demand is price inelastic when the percentage change in quantity demanded is
                  smaller than the percentage change in price (PED between 0 and −1).
                • Cigarettes are addictive, meaning that consumers will continue to buy them even
                  if the price rises, making demand price inelastic.
                • Cigarettes have few close substitutes, strengthening the inelasticity.
                • Smartphones, by contrast, have many competing brands — consumers can easily
                  switch from Apple to Samsung or Xiaomi if one brand raises its price, making
                  demand more price elastic.
                • Smartphones also take up a larger share of income than a single pack of
                  cigarettes, which tends to make demand more price elastic.
                • AO4
                • However, luxury or branded smartphones (e.g. iPhones) may have price inelastic
                  demand for loyal customers who view them as having no close substitute in their
                  view.
                • For young consumers starting to smoke, cigarette demand may actually be more
                  elastic as they have not yet developed an addiction.
                • In the long run, anti-smoking campaigns and substitute products (e.g. e-
                  cigarettes) make cigarette demand more elastic.
                • Smartphones sold under contract may have more inelastic demand than outright
                  purchases, because consumers are tied into multi-year agreements.
                • Overall, cigarettes are likely to have more price inelastic demand than
                  smartphones, though the difference depends on brand, consumer group and time
                  period.

Question       Calculate the monthly total costs of the clothing factory.                   Mark
number         Answer
4(a)           AO2 2 marks                                                                       (2)
               Award 1 mark for showing correct calculation
               $2,000 + $800 + (1,500 × $4.50)
               OR
               $2,000 + $800 + $6,750 (1)
               Award 1 mark for calculating correct total costs
               = $9,550 (1)
               Award 2 marks if total costs are correctly calculated as $9,550, even if
               no calculations are shown
               Do not award marks for the formula alone


Question       Analyse how internal economies of scale may lead to benefits for Zara.
number         Indicative content
4(b)                                        AO2 (3 marks) / AO3 (3 marks)
                • Internal economies of scale occur when average costs fall as a firm expands, such
                  as purchasing, marketing, technical, financial, managerial and risk-bearing
                  economies.
                • Zara has over 2,000 stores and produces large volumes of clothing, allowing it to
                  buy raw materials (e.g. fabric) in bulk at discounted prices — purchasing
                  economies.
                • Marketing costs (e.g. global campaigns) can be spread over many stores and
                  millions of garments, lowering the average marketing cost per item.
                • Large production runs allow Zara to invest in specialist machinery and centralised
                  distribution centres in Spain, generating technical economies and speeding up the
                  supply chain.
                • Lower average costs allow Zara to offer lower prices, maintain competitiveness
                  with rivals and earn higher profit margins.
                • Risk-bearing economies arise from operating in 96 countries — a poor season in
                  one market can be offset by strong sales elsewhere.

Question       Evaluate whether consumers are likely to benefit from the nationalisation of the
number         water supply company.
               Indicative content
4(c)                              AO2 (4 marks) / AO3 (4 marks) / AO4 (4 marks)
                • Nationalisation is the transfer of a private-sector firm into state ownership.
                • The Bolivian government would take over the water supply company currently
                  run by the private sector.
                • Under public ownership, the government may prioritise social objectives (e.g.
                  affordable access) rather than profit maximisation.
                • This could mean lower prices for water, particularly for poorer households, raising
                  their real incomes.
                • Water supply interruptions may be reduced because investment decisions can be
                  made in the long-term public interest rather than short-term shareholder returns.
                • Public ownership may also ensure that water, a necessity and merit good, is
                  provided equitably across the country.
                • AO4
                • However, the government may lack the expertise and resources to run the water
                  company efficiently, leading to higher unit costs and possibly worse service.
                • Without the profit motive, there may be less incentive to innovate or invest in
                  modern infrastructure, meaning quality declines over time.
                • Nationalisation requires significant public funding to buy out the private owners,
                  which creates an opportunity cost for other government spending (e.g. schools,
                  hospitals).
                • If the water company runs at a loss, taxpayers ultimately pay through higher taxes
                  — consumers as taxpayers may be worse off overall.
                • The outcome depends on the quality of government management, the size of any
                  price cuts, and whether investment in maintenance is sustained.
                • On balance, consumers may benefit from lower prices and more reliable supply in
                  the short run, but the long-run benefit depends on whether the government can
                  operate the company efficiently.


Level      Mark         Descriptor
           0            No rewardable material.
Level 1    1–4           • Demonstrates isolated elements of relevant knowledge and
                           understanding; may lack development of relevant points. Limited
                           application of economic terms, concepts, theories and calculations (AO2).
                         • Information presented will lack selectivity and organisation. Chain of
                           reasoning may be attempted. Interpretation of economic information will
                           be limited, with a lack of analysis of issues (AO3).
                         • Only offers one viewpoint, meaning there is an unbalanced and
                           incomplete evaluation, showing limited understanding and awareness. A
                           judgement or conclusion is unlikely to be attempted (AO4).

Level 2   5–8     • Demonstrates partial selectivity and some accurate knowledge and
                    understanding by developing some relevant points. Partial application of
                    economic terms, concepts, theories and calculations (AO2).
                  • Information presented will demonstrate some selectivity and
                    organisation. Chain of reasoning will be present but may be limited.
                    Interpretation of economic information will be good, with some analysis
                    of issues (AO3).

                    leading to an evaluation that may not demonstrate full understanding

                    fully supported (AO4).

Level 3   9–12    • Demonstrates specific and accurate knowledge and understanding by
                    developing relevant points. Appropriate application of economic terms,
                    concepts, theories and calculations (AO2).
                  • Information presented will demonstrate excellent selectivity and
                    organisation. Chain of reasoning will be coherent and logical.
                    Interpretation of economic information will be excellent with a thorough
                    analysis of issues (AO3).
                  • Offers more than one viewpoint. The argument is well balanced and
                    coherent, leading to an evaluation that demonstrates full understanding
                    and awareness. A supported judgement or conclusion is present (AO4).
